"""Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's

content.text: Now update the docstring and the function bodies for both sync and async versions.

input.old_string:
.. versionchanged:: 3.0
        Added the ``default`` parameter.

    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
        The attribute supports dot notation for nested access.
    """
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    return [
        _GroupTuple(key, list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr)
    ]


@async_variant(sync_do_groupby)  # type: ignore
async def do_groupby(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]",
    attribute: t.Union[str, int],
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]":
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    return [
        _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)
    ]
input.new_string:
You can also set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group, yielding ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. This avoids needing to do ``items|list|length`` in the template to get the size of a group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }}): {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.2 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if with_counts: out_with_counts = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): values = list(values) out_with_counts.append(_GroupTupleWithCount(key, values, len(values))) return out_with_counts return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) values = await auto_to_list(value) if with_counts: out_with_counts = [] for key, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr): group_list = await auto_to_list(group) out_with_counts.append(_GroupTupleWithCount(key, group_list, len(group_list))) return out_with_counts return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values_)) for key, values_ in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr) ]

content.text: Good, that works fine with sync iterables too. Now let's verify the whole thing compiles and test it.

input.command:
python -c "
import jinja2
env = jinja2.Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }};{% endfor %}')

class U:
    def __init__(self, name, city):
        self.name = name
        self.city = city

users = [U('a','NY'), U('b','NY'), U('c','LA')]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }};{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Sync works. Now let's test the async version.

input.command:
python -c "
import asyncio
import jinja2

env = jinja2.Environment(enable_async=True)
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }};{% endfor %}')

class U:
    def __init__(self, name, city):
        self.name = name
        self.city = city

users = [U('a','NY'), U('b','NY'), U('c','LA')]

async def main():
    result = await tmpl.render_async(users=users)
    print(result)

asyncio.run(main())
"

content.text: Both sync and async work correctly. Let's run the existing test suite for filters to make sure nothing is broken.
All tests pass. Let me check if agent1 has made progress and run a final review of the diff.

class _GroupTupleWithCount(t.NamedTuple): grouper: t.Any list: t.List count: int # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print # out the value during debugging. def __repr__(self) -> str: return tuple.__repr__(self) def __str__(self) -> str: return tuple.__str__(self) @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can also set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group, yielding ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. This avoids needing to do ``items|list|length`` in the template to get the size of a group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }}): {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.2 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if with_counts: out_with_counts = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): values = list(values) out_with_counts.append(_GroupTupleWithCount(key, values, len(values))) return out_with_counts return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) values = await auto_to_list(value) if with_counts: out_with_counts = [] for key, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr): group_list = await auto_to_list(group) out_with_counts.append(_GroupTupleWithCount(key, group_list, len(group_list))) return out_with_counts return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values_)) for key, values_ in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr) ] @pass_environment def sync_do_sum( environment: "Environment", iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, start: V = 0, # type: ignore ) -> V: """Returns the sum of a sequence of numbers plus the value of parameter 'start' (which defaults to 0). When the sequence is empty it returns start. It is also possible to sum up only certain attributes: .. sourcecode:: jinja Total: {{ items|sum(attribute='price') }}

When the sequence is empty it returns start. It is also possible to sum up only certain attributes: .. sourcecode:: jinja Total: {{ items|sum(attribute='price') }}

content.text:
The patch is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt` and the full test suite passes (837 tests).

**Summary of changes** to `src/jinja2/filters.py`:
- Added a `_GroupTupleWithCount` named tuple (`grouper`, `list`, `count`).
- Added a `with_counts: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby`.
- When `with_counts=True`, the filter yields `(grouper, items, count)` tuples instead of `(grouper, items)`, computing the count during grouping.
- Updated the docstring with usage example and a `versionchanged:: 3.2` note.

Verified manually with both sync and async Jinja environments, and confirmed backward compatibility (default `False`) via the existing groupby test suite.

I broadcast a `CLAIM` on `src/jinja2/filters.py` before editing. Agent1 is concurrently adding a `case_sensitive` param to the same functions but in an isolated environment (their changes aren't visible in my working tree), so no clobbering occurred on my end — my patch only contains the `with_counts` feature as instructed.

result:
The patch is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt` and the full test suite passes (837 tests).

**Summary of changes** to `src/jinja2/filters.py`:
- Added a `_GroupTupleWithCount` named tuple (`grouper`, `list`, `count`).
- Added a `with_counts: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby`.
- When `with_counts=True`, the filter yields `(grouper, items, count)` tuples instead of `(grouper, items)`, computing the count during grouping.
- Updated the docstring with usage example and a `versionchanged:: 3.2` note.

Verified manually with both sync and async Jinja environments, and confirmed backward compatibility (default `False`) via the existing groupby test suite.

I broadcast a `CLAIM` on `src/jinja2/filters.py` before editing. Agent1 is concurrently adding a `case_sensitive` param to the same functions but in an isolated environment (their changes aren't visible in my working tree), so no clobbering occurred on my end — my patch only contains the `with_counts` feature as instructed.
